family, be good-looking, of modest appearance or dress, truthful,
free from jealousy, impartial, with well-proportioned and strong
limbs and joints, undeformed, with beautiful hands, feet, nails,
eyes, chin, teeth, cars, forehead, brows and head, with handsome
body and a voice deep and sonorous. For, virtues and foibles are
generally dependent on good and bad physical features.
[The author here expects a true astrologer to be possessed of
all possible virtues and good physical features mentioned in the
smfrRrrar. We are reminded of the long list of virtues expected
of the —hero—of a drama according to Sanskrit rhetoricians.
Like Kalidasa our author is an adept in similes and general state-
ments. Here the last sentence is a beautiful instance of
He has put the statement of Kalidasa in his own words. See what
the poet has said in the KumUrasambhava Canto V-36 :
